body:
ACCESS ADVISORY COMMITTEE  
M I N U T E S 
 
Wednesday, February 18, 2026, 5:00 pm  
Council Chamber, City Hall  
4949 Canada Way, Burnaby, BC  
 
PRESENT:  Councillor Maita Santiago, Chair  
 Councillor Richard T. Lee, Vice Chair  
 Councillor Daniel Tetrault, Member  
 Odette Brassard, Resident Representative  
 Karim Damani, Resident Representative  
 Mario Gregorio, Resident Representative  
 Suzanne Norman, Resident Representative  (participated electronically)  
 Sonny Senghera , Resident Representative  
 Anna Wijesinghe, Resident Representative  
  
ABSENT:  Rod Bitz, Resident Representative  
  
STAFF:  Amy Choh, Director Engineering Transportation  
 Vishad Deeplaul, Senior Manager EDI  (participated electronically)  
 Peter Buono, Manager Recreation Facilities  
 Kerry Teaves , Manager Recreation Services  
 Andre Kolber, Manager Parks Operations (participated electronically)  
 Noreen Ma, Manager Community Development  
 Kyra Lubell, Planner 3  
 Claire Shepansky, Planner 1  
 Denise Chak, Administrative Officer 1  
 1. CALL TO ORDER  
The Chair, Councillor Santiago, called the Committee meeting to order at 5:00 pm. 
 2. TERRITORIAL ACKNOWLEDGEMENT  
Chair Santiago respectfully acknowledged that the City of Burnaby is located on the 
unceded territories of the xʷm əθkʷəy̓əm (Musqueam), S ḵwx̱wú7mesh (Squamish), 
səlilwətaɬ (Tsleil- Waututh), and kʷikʷ əƛ̓əm (Kwikwetlem) Peoples. Each Nation has 

Access Advisory Committee Meeting  
Minutes February 18, 2026  
 
 2 distinct histories and distinct traditional territories that fully or partially encompass the 
city. The City encourages you to learn more about the Host Nations whose ancestors have occupied and used these lands, including parts of present -day Burnaby, for  
thousands of years.  
 
3. ADOPTION OF AGENDA 
3.1 Access Advisory Committee Agenda for February 18, 2026  
MOVED AND SECONDED THAT the agenda for the Access Advisory Committee meeting of February 18, 
2026 be adopted, as presented.  
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY  
 
4. ADOPTION OF MINUTES  
4.1 Minutes of the Access Advisory Committee meeting held on October 1, 
2025  
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the minutes of the Access Advisory Committee meeting held on October 1, 
2025, be now adopted.  
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY  
 
5. DELEGATIONS AND INVITED PRESENTATIONS  
5.1 Delegation: Dementia Co -Creation Academy - Co-Creation through 
Recreation: Crafting Dementia Inclusive Communities  
Speaker:  Farhad Farhangfar, Co- Founder, Dementia Co- Creation Academy  
Farhad Farhangfar, Co -Founder, Dementia Co- Creation Academy , appeared 
before the Committee to share information about their community -led initiative 
that started two years ago in Burnaby . The initiative aims to enhance social 
citizenship for people living with dementia and reduce stigma by bring ing 
volunteers and people with lived experience together to participate in  recreational 
activities, art projects and art -based therapeutic practices.  
The speaker noted that dementia inclusion is increasingly important as 
diagnoses rise globally and emphasized the value of fostering understanding and 
connection to build more inclusive communities.  
Access Advisory Committee Meeting  
Minutes February 18, 2026  
 
 3 6. ADMINISTRATIVE REPORTS  
No Administrative Reports were received.  
 
7. INFORMATION REPORTS  
7.1 VERBAL REPORT: ACCESSIBILITY FEEDBACK FORM REPORTING -  
SEPTEMBER TO DECEMBER 2025  
Claire Shepansky, Planner 1, provided a verbal report on submissions received 
through the Accessibility Feedback Form for the period of September to December 2025 . During this period,  a total of 28 comments were received across 
the following five (5) categories:  
• Crosswalks and sidewalks: twelve (12)  
• Civic buildings and facilities: two (6)  
• Other: three (3)  
• Parks and outdoor space: three  (3) 
• Adapted activities and programs: one (1)  
• Customer service: one (1)  
• Public washrooms: one (1)  
• Accessibility Plan: one (1)  
C. Shepansky  further  noted that , overall in 2025,  150 comments were received 
through the Accessibility Feedback Form,  of which 130 were applicable and 20 
were deemed not applicable. Monthly trends indicated that periods with higher  
volumes  of feedback  aligned with promotion al efforts undertaken by the 
Marketing and Communications team.  
In conclusion, the speaker outlined how feedback is reviewed and actioned and shared an example of work completed as  a direct  result of feedback received via 
the Accessibility Feedback Form.  The Marketing and Communications team will 
initiate a second round of outreach to further promote the form.  
 
8. CORRESPONDENCE  
No items of correspondence were received.  
 9. OTHER BUSINESS  
9.1 Adapted Activities at City Facilities  
Access Advisory Committee Meeting  
Minutes February 18, 2026  
 
 4 Representative Senghera noted the Access Advisory Committee has been 
focusing on several key themes, including transportation infrastructure, 
education/awareness, and facilities.  With respect to facilities,  they inquired  about 
the City’s adapted activities that allow youth and adults with disabilities to access 
programming at municipal facilities, specifically:  
• what activities are provided?  
• how do Burnaby’s offerings compare to other municipalities?  
• how has participation been trending?  
• how are these programs promoted and measured?  
• what policies are in place to remove barriers to access?  
• what plans are in place for future enhancements and community input?  
Staff advised that the City’s Parks, Recreation and Culture (PRC)  department 
offers a range of adapted and inclusive recreation opportunities, including 
one-to-one supports for preschoolers, children, and youth, inclusive camps, 
adaptive skating and swimming lessons (including ASL interpretation), sensory -adapted programs, and partnerships such as with the Canucks Autism 
Network. Additional offerings include social and targeted programming for 
pre -teens and adults, as well as drop- in recreation opportunities . 
For older adults, adapted and inclusive options include gentle fitness and balance classes, chair yoga, mobility programs, adapted AquaFit, active aging 
fitness programs, and some seniors’ bus trips with accessible transportation.  
Staff also highlighted facility and service supports, including accessible change rooms and washrooms, pool lifts, accessible pool entry points, staff inclusion training, and partnership programming with the BC Provincial School for the Deaf.  
In conclusion, s taff noted that Burnaby’s  adapted  recreation offerings are high 
compared to other municipalities, with continued demand for one- to-one 
supports. Adapted programs are promoted through the seasonal activity guide, the City’s website, and social media channels.  
 
9.2 Tactile Markings at Brentwood Town Centre  
Representative Damani in quired whether the tactile markings adjacent to the 
floating bus stop and bike lane at Brentwood Town Centre have been changed or 
removed, noting concerns that they may have been i ncorrectly  directing uses  and 
presenting potential safety issues.  
Access Advisory Committee Meeting  
Minutes February 18, 2026  
 
 5 Staff advised the design of the tactile markings at Brentwood Town Centre 
follows the TransLink design guide for bus stops adjacent to cycling infrastructure. The guide was developed by TransLink and the BC Ministry of 
Transportation and Transit  in 2024.  
With the project now complete, the Transportation team is arranging a site visit to seek  further  feedback from the Access Advisory Committee, the Canadian 
National Institute for the Blind (CNIB), and TransLink  on the installed measures.  
 
9.3 Creating Opportunities to Alleviate Loneliness and Foster Social 
Connections  
Representative Gregorio acknowledged the collective role of the City, 
policymakers, community partners, and academia in advancing accessible 
facilities and programs that strengthen social connections among residents. They  
also shared an example illustrating that participation in weekly ukulele classes 
supported functional improvement and increased community engagement for an individual living with dementia, highlighting the value of community arts programs 
in helping parti cipants remain connected.  
 
10. ADJOURNMENT  
By unanimous consent and without objection, the Committee meeting adjourned at 6:25 
pm. 
  
   
Councillor Maita Santiago  
CHAIR  Denise Chak  
ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICER

---

None.

Purpose: To seek feedback from Access Advisory Committee members on topics for a disability‑focused awareness campaign. Staff presentation by Frances Tang-Graham, Acting Director, Communications and Marketing

Burnaby Disability 
Awareness Campaign
Marketing & Communications Campaign Proposal
AAC April 15, 2026
Accessibility Plan
•Action 3.1: Create and share disability focused awareness 
programming that highlights the voices of people with disabilities. 
•Received funding from SPARC BC grant for honorariums.
Campaign Goals
•Reduce ableism in our community, especially in and around City- run 
facilities, events, and services.
•Challenge stereotypes and address misconceptions about disability 
and access.
•Promote how to contact City staff about accessibility concerns and accommodations at our facilities, events, and in our services.
Campaign Details  
Material to produce: 
•3-4 educational social media reels (~1 minute) featuring people in our 
community with disabilities sharing their life experiences - how long they have 
lived with disability, how it has impacted their lives, the challenges and barriers 
they overcome daily and how accessibility changes improve them.
•Supporting infographics  highlighting the key messages and important data 
behind each video.
•Digital screens  in civic facilities ( e.g. libraries, @ city events) addressing 
common challenges ( e.g. invisible disability, sensory overstimulation).
•Improvements to City of Burnaby website  addressing accessibility in City 
spaces and directing concerns to the Accessibility Feedback Form. 
Campaign Details  
In-market dates:
•Start May 25 (beginning of Access Ability Week)
•Digital spending ends Aug 31 (end of summer/peak usage of City facilities and 
events)
•Organic/unpaid promotion continues as evergreen content
Budget:
•Honoraria for participants $500
•Digital promotion/spending $500
•Total: $1,000
Campaign Strategy 
•Use a storytelling  approach that highlights real people’s  lived 
experiences.
•Focus on fewer, high- quality  communications that will last and make 
an impression - too many can be tuned out! 
•Engage with participatory, interactive prompts , e.g. “spot the 
barrier”
•Focus on digital communications - COB’s largest audience and the 
most cost -effective way to spend our advertising budget.
Campaign Details  
Workback :
•Apr 15 –  AA committee meeting to approve topics
•Apr 21 – Call for participants for videos (honoraria provided) – ask committee
•May 15 –  Participants selected. Each provided with prompts/guide questions 
for their voiceover.  
•May 29  – Deadline for participants to provide content (photos, video) 
•Jun 5 – 1st proofs of social content 
•Jun 12 – Final proofs of social content
•Jun 19 – Supporting infographics created for review
•Jul 6 – Campaign launch
Campaign Topics
•Committee to advise on priorities for subjects covered.
•For max impact, suggest we choose 3 or 4 and concentrate our 
messaging - public attention is short!
•To make an impression, our communications should be focused and 
brief - under 1 minute, and ideally around 30 seconds.
•Focus on city spaces and services-  our staff resources need to be 
allocated to COB -related projects.
Campaign Topics
Think about sparking engagement  and opportunities to participate! 
What stories should we highlight in this campaign? 
»For example: 
•Types of disability & invisible disabilities
•Guide dog handler rights and guide dog etiquette
•Sensory overstimulation and low- sensory areas
•Barriers beyond physical access: attitudinal, misconceptions, assumptions, 
technology
•Language and how we speak about disability

Purpose: To provide Committee with information on the completed Social Infrastructure Needs Assessment. Staff presentation by Erin Rennie, Planner 3.

Burnaby 2050 
Official 
Community Plan
+
Social Infrastructure 
Needs Assessment
Access Advisory Committee
April 15, 2026
Agenda
1.What is Burnaby 2050? 
•Growth and Change
•OCP Overview
•Policy Framework
•Plan Highlights 
2.Social Infrastructure Needs 
Assessment
•Public Schools Profile 
•Community Resource Centres
Profile 
•Next Steps 

Burnaby 2050
•Burnaby’s new Official Community Plan (OCP) 
•A long -term vision for the community. 
•Provides the strategic direction, framework and 
guidance to meet the anticipated needs of the 
community while ensuring Burnaby remains livable.
•Comprehensive policy plan covering all planning topics 
and a long -range land use plan to guide development 
decisions. 
•2022 -2025: robust multi -phase engagement process
•Adopted December 2025

Growth and Change
Growth Projections 
Forecasted Numbers 
2021 2030 2040 2050Growth 
(2021 -
2050)
Population 261,810 311,510 361,630 408,150 +55.9%
Housing 
Units106,170 128,330 151,860 174,060 +63.9%
Jobs 160,330 185,490 209,940 231,820 +44.6%Key drivers of change 
•A growing and aging 
population 
•Housing affordability and 
housing options 
•Climate change 
•A changing economy
•Immigration
•Aging infrastructure 
•Provincial housing policy 
OCP Overview 
Vision
“Burnaby is a green and inclusive city with safe and connected neighbourhoods and 
a vibrant economy where people want to live, work and play for generations to come.”
Three 
Lenses
OCP Overview 
The Plan at a Glance Vision
Implementation
Land Use Framework Policy Framework
OCP Overview
Land Use Framework 
The Land Use Framework guides development to meet 
Burnaby’s anticipated residential, employment, 
environmental and community needs. Notably, the Land 
Use Framework uses a parcel -based Land Use Map. 

Policy Framework 
OCP Goals 
Goal 1: Foster complete communities that are sustainable, equitable and accessible to everyone 
Goal 2: Create inclusive public spaces that are accessible and respond to everyday needs 
Goal 3: Support a diverse and thriving economy 
Goal 4: Create and maintain efficient and well -connected transportation and infrastructure networks 
Goal 5: Lead in climate action and protect, enhance and restore the natural environment 
Plan Highlights
Tenant 
protectionTransit -oriented 
developmentHousing choices
Transportation 
connections
New 
neighbourhood 
centresAmenities to 
support growth
Welcoming 
public spacesEnvironmental 
protection …and more
Actions for 
reconciliationSupport for small 
businesses
Social Infrastructure Needs 
Assessment
What is Social Infrastructure (SI) for Burnaby
Social infrastructure are 
facilities and spaces that 
are used for social and 
community services, 
activities and events 
delivered by the City, non -
profits, private sector and 
other community operators 
for groups and individuals . 
Outcomes:
•Health and well -being
•Social connection and 
belonging
•Daily needs 
•Life-long learning
Type of Social Infrastructure
•Child care centres
•Community Resource Centres (CRCs)
•Public libraries
•Arts and culture facilities *
•Recreation facilities *
•Parks * 
•Public schools
•Healthcare facilities
•Commercial & retail spaces
*These SI types under the Parks, Recreation and Cultural portfolio 
will be included in Making Life Better: Parks, Recreation and 
Culture Plan . 

Project Background & Methodology
•Project rationale: 
•SI should be planned to keep pace with growth
•Inform the OCP, other future City strategies
•Data Gathering, including:
•Best Practice Review
•Mapping
•Engagement Data with Community Partners 
•Identification of needs and gaps across Burnaby based on:
•Infrastructure
•Population
•Daily Needs 
•Transportation
•SI findings were put into profiles that tried to answer: 
•Priorities per quadrant and Community Plan Area
•Amount of floor space is required to meet the needs
•How to meet the needs
Engagement Findings
Challenges 
•Affordability 
•Suitability 
•Need for more 
program space and 
rental spaceOpportunities 
•Locate new SI in places 
convenient to transit
•Foster co -location 
•More passive spaces for 
informal gatherings and social 
connectionsDesign Considerations 
•Multi -purpose spaces 
•Space for storage to support 
co-location 
•Informal spaces for gathering 
that do not require bookings 
(e.g., picnic tables, lobbies) 
•Dedicated spaces for youth 
within a facility 
•Accessible design for public 
washrooms and 
accommodating mobility 
devices 
Accessibility Considerations in SINA 
Engagement 
Consultation with local accessibility/disability -
focused organizations including BACI, 
posAbilities , BC Centre for Abilities. 
Findings and Recommendations 
•Locating services and amenities near 
SkyTrain and frequent transit 
•Facility design considerations such as 
accessible washrooms and 
accommodation for wheelchairs and 
mobility devices 

Summary of Quadrant Prioritization

Profile Extract: Community Resource Centres
Strategic Recommendations
In conjunction with the Social Sustainability Strategy and Burnaby 2050 OCP, 
the City may consider: 
1.Explore creating a service provision target for CRCs. 
2.Encourage and support on -site CRCs for City -owned facilities delivered 
through Community Benefit Bonus. 
3.Explore and confirm ideal minimum size for CRCs. 
4.Create design standards for CRCs. 
5.Conduct research on co -location facility models and administration 
models for CRCs. 
6.Explore capacity -building opportunities or partnerships with non -profits 
to build literacy, knowledge and strategic planning for organizational 
facility/space needs. 
Next Steps –Social Infrastructure Needs Assessment
OCP Implementation
•Develop OCP Implementation Action Plan 
(implementation actions & timeframe, monitoring 
framework, reporting plan)
•5 year updates
Social Infrastructure Needs Assessment : 
•Inform the City of Burnaby’s amenity framework 
and implementation strategy (Community Amenity 
Strategy).
•Internal staff resource for planning, reporting etc.

Thank you

Purpose: To provide the Committee with an update on submissions received through the Accessibility Feedback Form from January to March 2026.

---

None.

briefs.title: Council to consider: Burnaby disability awareness campaign topics and accessibility updates
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to meet on April 15, 2026 to give feedback on a proposed city-run disability awareness campaign planned to launch during National AccessAbility Week (starting May 25). The committee will also receive updates on accessibility feedback submissions, a completed social infrastructure study, and ongoing projects like the Brentwood Town Centre accessibility pilot and accessibility measures for the 2026 municipal election.
briefs.action_required: If you serve customers with disabilities, watch for the city's upcoming awareness campaign — it may affect how you communicate accessibility at your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby disability awareness campaign — call for participants opens
Burnaby disability awareness campaign — National AccessAbility Week launch
Burnaby disability awareness campaign — digital promotion ends

briefs.title: Council to consider: Burnaby Access Advisory Committee minutes on inclusion and accessibility programs
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving the minutes from the Access Advisory Committee meeting held on February 18, 2026. At that meeting, the committee reviewed accessibility feedback from residents, heard about dementia-inclusive recreation programs, and discussed adapted activities at city facilities and tactile safety markings at Brentwood Town Centre.
briefs.action_required: Watch for council's approval — accessibility changes at city facilities may affect your customers or staff.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to Consider Administrative Reports — April 15, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review administrative reports at its upcoming April 15, 2026 meeting. No details about the specific reports have been provided, so the full scope of what Council will consider — and any impact on local businesses — is not yet known.
briefs.action_required: Check the Burnaby Council agenda for report details before the April 15, 2026 meeting.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council administrative reports meeting date

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign Launching May 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to review and provide feedback on a proposed disability-focused awareness campaign at its April 15, 2026 meeting. The campaign would run from late May through August 2026, using short social media videos, infographics, and digital screens in city facilities to reduce ableism and challenge misconceptions about disability — with a total proposed budget of $1,000.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the committee's topic decisions — your business may be featured or referenced in city-run disability awareness content.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — Access Advisory Committee feedback meeting
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — call for video participants opens
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — National AccessAbility Week launch date
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — digital promotion end date

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign Seeks Community Input on Topics
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to consider a proposed disability awareness campaign that would run from late May through August 2026. The campaign would feature short videos and social media content highlighting real people's experiences with disability, with a total budget of $1,000 and a focus on City-run spaces and services.
briefs.action_required: If you or someone you know wants to participate in the campaign videos, watch for the call for participants around April 21.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — call for video participants opens
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — participant selection deadline
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — campaign launch date
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — digital promotion spending ends

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's Social Infrastructure Needs Assessment for Long-Term Growth Planning
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review a completed Social Infrastructure Needs Assessment (SINA) — a study that maps out what community spaces and services (like childcare centres, community resource centres, and public schools) the city will need as its population grows toward 408,000 by 2050. The findings are expected to shape how Burnaby plans and funds community amenities going forward, including through its new Official Community Plan (OCP) adopted in December 2025.
briefs.action_required: Watch for how the SINA shapes Burnaby's Community Amenity Strategy — it may affect development requirements near your business.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Accessibility Feedback Form Update for January–March 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Accessibility Committee is set to receive a verbal update on complaints and suggestions submitted through the City's Accessibility Feedback Form between January and March 2026. This report is informational — it gives councillors a snapshot of what residents flagged about accessibility barriers, but no new rules or requirements for businesses are being voted on at this time.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: No Correspondence Items on Burnaby's April 2026 Agenda
briefs.summary: The April 15, 2026 Burnaby City Council agenda includes a correspondence section, but no items or letters have been listed under it. There is nothing for Council to review or act on in this section at this time.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign and Accessibility Pilot Projects
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to meet on April 15, 2026, to consider several upcoming accessibility initiatives — including a proposed disability awareness campaign planned to launch during National AccessAbility Week (May 25), an accessibility pilot project at Brentwood Town Centre, and accessibility measures for the 2026 municipal election. No final decisions have been made yet.
briefs.action_required: Watch for outcomes from the April 15 meeting — accessibility changes at Brentwood Town Centre may affect nearby businesses.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — proposed launch date (National AccessAbility Week)
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — digital promotion end date
Access Advisory Committee meeting — accessibility initiatives up for consideration

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign Launching National AccessAbility Week
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to review and provide feedback on a proposed disability awareness campaign at its April 15, 2026 meeting. The campaign would run short videos and social media content featuring real community members with disabilities, with a planned launch around May 25, 2026 — the start of National AccessAbility Week — and a total budget of $1,000.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the committee's decision — the campaign launches May 25, 2026 and may affect how customers interact with your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — proposed launch date
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — digital promotion end date

briefs.title: Council to consider: Burnaby disability awareness campaign launching May 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to review and provide input on a proposed disability-focused awareness campaign. The campaign would run from late May through August 2026, using short social media videos, digital screens in city buildings, and website updates to reduce ableism — that is, discrimination against people with disabilities — and help residents report accessibility concerns to the City.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the committee's topic decisions — your customers or staff with disabilities may be featured or affected.

key_dates.label: Burnaby disability awareness campaign — call for video participants opens
Burnaby disability awareness campaign — launch date (National AccessAbility Week)
Burnaby disability awareness campaign — digital promotion ends

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Brentwood Town Centre Accessibility Pilot Project and Disability Awareness Campaign
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to discuss two upcoming initiatives at its April 15, 2026 meeting: a pilot project to improve accessibility at Brentwood Town Centre, and a proposed disability awareness campaign planned to launch during National AccessAbility Week. The awareness campaign would use short social media videos and digital content featuring real community members with disabilities, with a total proposed budget of $1,000.
briefs.action_required: Watch the April 15 committee meeting — decisions here could affect accessibility requirements near your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — proposed launch date
Burnaby Access Advisory Committee meeting — Brentwood accessibility pilot discussed
National AccessAbility Week — Burnaby campaign digital promotion begins
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — digital spending ends

briefs.title: Council to Consider Accessibility Measures for Burnaby's 2026 General Local Election
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to discuss accessibility measures for the 2026 General Local Election at its April 15, 2026 meeting. The committee will consider what supports and accommodations should be in place to make voting accessible to residents with disabilities — no final decision has been made yet.
briefs.action_required: Watch for updates on the 2026 election accessibility measures if your business serves customers with disabilities.

key_dates.label: Access Advisory Committee meeting — 2026 election accessibility measures discussed
